description: Lock direct archaeological scope, chronology, component direction, and removal of generic topic-as-prerequisite edges across the textile, leather, shelter, and early-mechanism batch.

checks.reason: The Ohalo II inserts predate the later generic sickle node.
No wooden Ohalo II handle survives and the assemblage includes hand-held blades.
Manufactured flint inserts constitute the direct evidence.
The rounded date remains anchored to the 23,000-year-old Ohalo II context.
The spindle interpretation does not establish a basketry lineage.
The possible whorls predate the scoped preserved weaving evidence.
The Nahal Ein-Gev II artifacts are perforated stones.
The rounded date remains anchored to the 12,000-year-old Late Natufian context.
The scoped fibers are locally gathered tree bast rather than a domesticated crop.
The scoped woven fibers are plant bast rather than wool.
The Catalhoyuk textiles remain anchored to the 6700-6500 cal BCE range.
The Dzudzuana evidence is colored fiber rather than woven cloth.
Plant material does not establish a medicinal-practice dependency.
The rounded date remains anchored to the paper's 30,000-year age.
The component-to-system direction must not be reversed.
Clay or stone warp weights are not pottery vessels.
Spinning and warp tension are separate textile operations.
Ulucak loom-weight use remains anchored to about 6000 BCE.
No wooden frame survives in the scoped Ulucak context.
The grouped weights are constitutive components of the reconstructed loom.
The loom reconstruction remains anchored to Ulucak Level IVb.
Dyeing is not required for the room 28 workshop claim.
Forty-seven spindle whorls directly evidence yarn production.
Room 28 remains anchored to the 1700-1595 BCE radiocarbon range.
Tx1 is nalbinding rather than woven cloth.
Indigotin is chemically identified in the textile.
Tx1 remains anchored to the 1915-1745 BCE range.
Managed animals are only one possible hide source.
No advanced stone implement is required by the chemical tanning evidence.
The evidenced method is aqueous plant-extract tanning rather than smoke tanning.
Tanning is a constitutive stage of broader hide-to-leather processing.
The Schnidejoch leather remains anchored to the 2914-2652 cal BCE range.
The node is a specific Roman panel artifact rather than a universal shelter stage.
The scoped evidence contains no analyzed wooden frame.
Manufactured leather is constitutive of the surviving tent panels.
The panels remain anchored to Vindolanda's circa 85-90 CE first occupation.
Q'eswachaka uses grass cable and stone abutments rather than timber.
The structural ropes are twisted and braided by hand.
The tradition remains anchored conservatively to more than six centuries of Inka technique.
The basic plain bearing is a wooden axle supported in the chassis.
The mechanism remains anchored to the Ljubljana wheel-and-axle range.
The Yanghai artifact is directly identified as a riding saddle.
The study directly supports leather working but not a specific tanning method.
The saddle is stitched hide rather than a rope structure.
The saddle improves an established riding practice.
Shaped and stitched leather constitutes the artifact.
The saddle remains anchored to the 727-396 BCE radiocarbon range.
The cited shelter evidence is built from plant material.
The rag-wick lamp remains anchored to the museum's 1070-945 BCE or earlier range.
